questions.question: Ananda Puraskhar award is given to literatures of which language?
questions.explanation: Ananda Puraskar is the major state based annual award given to the literary personalities for extensive contributions in Bengali literature and language. It was constituted on April 20, 1958 by ABP Group. These awards are given in memory of Anandabazar Patrika founders, Prafulla Kumar Sarkar and Suresh Chandra Majumdar.

questions.question: In which year was the first Sahitya Akademi awards presented?
questions.explanation: The Sahitya Akademi was formally inaugurated by the Government of India on 12 March 1954 and the first award was presented in the year 1955. Also referred to as India's National Academy of Letters, is the central institution for literary dialogue, publication and promotion in the country and the only institution that undertakes literary activities in 24 Indian languages, including English.

questions.question: Which among the following fellowship is conferred on writers of SAARC nations?
questions.explanation: Premchand Fellowship is given to scholars doing research on Indian literature or to creative writers from the countries of the SAARC region other than India. The first Premchand Fellowship was offered to Janab Intizar Husain eminent Urdu fiction writer and columnist from Pakistan.

questions.question: Which among the following award is considered as Nobel Prize of Asia?
questions.explanation: The Ramon Magsaysay Award is regarded as Nobel Prize of Asia. It is named after Ramon Magsaysay, the seventh President of the Philippines. The Ramon Magsaysay Award Foundation gives the prize to Asian individuals achieving excellence in their respective fields. The prize was established in 1957.

questions.question: Who was the first individual to be awarded Bharat Ratna posthumously?
questions.explanation: Lal Bahadur Shastri became the first individual to be honoured with Bharat Ratna posthumously. The Bharat Ratna is the highest civilian award in India. It was constituted in the year 1954. First recipients of the Bharat Ratna were C. Rajagopalachari, S.Radhakrishnan and C. V. Raman.

questions.question: Nobel Prizes were first awarded in which year?
questions.explanation: The first Nobel Prize Award Ceremony was held in 1901. The first Nobel Prizes are awarded in Stockholm, Sweden, in the fields of physics, chemistry, medicine, literature, and peace. Each recipient, receives a gold medal, a diploma, and a sum of money, which is decided by the Nobel Foundation.

questions.question: Dadasaheb Phalke Award is associated with which field?
questions.explanation: It is India's highest award in cinema. The recipient is honored for their outstanding contribution to the growth and development of Indian cinema. To honor the Father of Indian Cinema, Dhundiraj Govind Phalke. It was introduced in 1969 by the government. The award comprises of a Swarna Kamal, a cash Prize of Rupees Ten Lakhs and a shawl.

questions.question: Man Booker Prize was established in which year?
questions.explanation: The Man Booker Prize was established in 1969. It is a literary prize awarded each year for the best original novel, written in the English language and published in the UK. The winner receives ≤50,000 as well as the ≤2,500 awarded to each of the shortlisted authors. Both the winner and the shortlisted authors are guaranteed a worldwide readership plus an increase in book sales.

questions.question: The first Indian author to receive the Man Booker Prize was conferred the title for which of the following work?
questions.explanation: Arundathi Roy was the first Indian citizen to receive Man Booker Prize in the year 1997, for her work "The God of small things". It was her debut novel. Her recent works are the Ministry of Utmost happiness, The Capitalism: The Ghost Story etc.

questions.question: Who was the first Indian to win Oscar award?
questions.explanation: Bhanu Athaiya became the first Indian to win an Academy Award in 1983, for designing the costumes for Richard Attenborough's Gandhi. It is a set of twenty-four awards for artistic and technical merit in the American film industry. The award was first presented in the year 1929.

questions.question: "Shikhara" is predominately found in which form of architecture?
questions.explanation: Shikharas are is predominately found in Nagara style of architecture. It is popular form of architecture in Northern India. The tower of the temple in nagara style is called as shikharas. They are tall and curvilinear in shape. Shikhara is basically of two types: (1) the latina, curvilinear in outline, the type most usually found above the sanctuary; and (2) the phamsana, rectilinear in outline and capped by a bell-shaped member, the form more usually found above the mandapa.

questions.question: Cire- pedru is related to which of the following?
questions.explanation: Cire pedru is also known as lost-wax process is used in bronze sculptures. In Indian subcontinent, this process originated in Indus Valley culture. Still today many tribal communities use this technique. In this molten metal is poured into a mold that has been created by means of a wax model. And once the molten metal has solidified, the outer shell will be removed.

questions.question: The term "Tirthankaras" are related to which among the following religion?
questions.explanation: Thirthankaras are the religious gurus of Jainism. There are totally 24 thirthankaras. The first tirthankara was Rishabhanatha and the last being Mahavira. They are regarded spiritual teacher of the dharma. Their teachings form the basis for Jainism. According to Jain belief, each cosmic age produces its own group of 24 Tirthankaras at different intervals.

questions.question: Construction of last storey of Qutub Minar was completed by which of the following ruler?
questions.explanation: Construction of last storey was completed by Firuz shah Tughlaq. Originally the construction of Minar was started by Qutb Ud Din Aibak, but completion was done by Firuz by adding another storey. Qutub Minar is situated in Delhi. It is one of the UNESCO World Heritage sites of India. It is made of red sandstone and marble, with five storeys. The diameter of the minaret reduces from bottom to top like minaret of Jam in Afghanistan.

questions.question: "Jatakas" are related to which of the following religions?
questions.explanation: Jatakas are Buddhists literature which deals with lives of Buddha in the past births. These stories form the base for paintings in Ajantha caves and other Buddhists paintings. These stories are in didactic form. The Buddha himself used jataka stories to explain concepts like karma and rebirth and to emphasize the importance of certain moral values.

questions.question: Bharat Mata painting was created in which year?
questions.explanation: Bharat Mata was painted by Abanindranath Tagore in 1905. It was one of the earliest depictions of Bharat Mata. It was painted in response to partition of Bengal in 1905. It depicts a saffron clad woman, dressed like a sadhvi, holding a book, sheaves of paddy, a piece of white cloth and a garland in her four hands.

questions.question: Pushkar Mela is held in which of the following states?
questions.explanation: Pushkar is a town in Rajasthan where annual camel fair is organized. The Fair begins on Kartik Shukla Ekadashi and ends in Kartik Poornima. The fair is held near the banks of Pushkar lake. It is also famous for livestock trading and camel race.

questions.question: Chaita Parva festival is celebrated in which of the following states?
questions.explanation: It is a tribal festival of koraput, Odisha. It is celebrated by Bhuyians tribe of Odisha. For the whole month of Chaitra the tribal people remain in festive mood. Singing, dancing and hunting forms the major part of the festival. It is also celebrated in parts of Bihar.

questions.question: Bhand Pather is theater form of which Indian state?
questions.explanation: It is the traditional theatre form of Kashmir, is a unique combination of dance, music and acting. It is preferred for inducing laughter. Music is provided with surnai, nagaara and dhol. Since the actors of Bhand Pather are mainly from the farming community, the impact of their way of living, ideals and sensitivity is discernible.

questions.question: Which among the following states doesn't have tradition of string puppets form of art?
questions.explanation: The ancient tradition of string puppets or marionettes having jointed limbs controlled by strings allow far greater flexibility. It is famous in Rajasthan, Orissa, Karnataka and Tamil Nadu. Puppetry throughout the ages has held an important place in traditional entertainment. Like traditional theatre, themes for puppet theatre are mostly based on epics and legends.

questions.question: Who was the author of the book "The Argumentative Indian"?
questions.explanation: The book was written by Amartya Sen. It deals with India's history and identity, focusing on the traditions of public debate and intellectual pluralism. Amartya Sen is an Indian economist and a Nobel laurate. It tries to outline the need to understand contemporary India in the light of its long argumentative tradition.

questions.question: The Discovery of India is the work of who among the following?
questions.explanation: The Discovery of India was written by Jawaharlal Nehru. It deals with rich cultural heritage of India, its history and its philosophy as seen through the eyes of a patriot fighting for the independence of his country. It was written during Nehru's imprisonment. It was adapted into the Indian television series titled Bharat Ek Khoj, released in 1988 by director Shyam Benegal.

questions.question: "The life Tree" was authored by who among the following?
questions.explanation: It was authored by A.P.J. Abdul Kalam. It is a collection of poems about India and it rich culture. It also gives a deep understanding of the problems of Indian society.

questions.question: In which of the following works was India's National Song published?
questions.explanation: Anandamath was written by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee and published in 1882. The national song of India, Vande Mataram, was first published in this novel. The novel was banned by the British. The ban was lifted later by the Government of India after independence.

questions.question: Which among the following work relating to India's freedom struggle was NOT authored by an Indian?
questions.explanation: Awakening was authored by A.O.Hume, who served as an British-India Civil servant. He was also instrumental in formations of Indian National Congress. It deals about failed promises of British to India.

questions.question: Which among the following is a work of Subash Chandra Bose?
questions.explanation: Indian Struggle was the work of S.C. Bose. It covers the history of the Indian independence movement to end British imperial rule over India. It gives an analysis of Non-cooperation movement, civil disobedience and quit India movements.

questions.question: Which of the following books has won Man Booker Prize?
questions.explanation: These are works of Indian Authors, which has received Man Booker Prize. It is a literary prize awarded each year for the best original novel, written in the English language and published in the UK.

questions.question: Which among the following book is NOT an autobiography?
questions.explanation: The book describes individualism as a religion and paint a reality that is primarily virtual, rather than physical. It was written by Alexander Bard and Jan Söderqvist. It talks about effects of digital dynamics on various levels of a globalized world.

questions.question: Who among the following has rendered the English translation of kalidasa's Malavikagnimitram?
questions.explanation: Kalidasa's Malavikagnimitram was translated into English by C.H.Tawney. The first edition of translation was published in 1875 and second edition in 1891. He was an English educator and scholar, primarily known for his translations of Sanskrit classics into English.

questions.question: Which among the following is NOT an autobiography of former US Presidents?
questions.explanation: With malice toward none is a biography on Abraham Lincoln. It was written by Stephen B. Oates. The author, a historian, uses primary and secondary sources to illuminate the personal and professional life of Abraham Lincoln.
